title: Quantum Algorithms for Triangle Cut Sparsification
abstract: Triangles capture higher-order structures in graphs and are fundamental to applications such as clustering and network analysis.   To enable efficient use of such structures at scale, we study the problem of \emph{triangle cut sparsification}, which aims to reduce the graph size while approximately preserving triangle counts across every cut.   We investigate \emph{quantum algorithms} for this problem, using triangle listing as our main technical ingredient.   In particular, we present a quantum algorithm for triangle listing that, for a graph with $n$ vertices, $m$ edges, and $t$ triangles, runs in time $T_{\mathrm{q\text{-}list}} =$ $\widetilde{O}\bigl(\min(n^{5/4}t^{7/12} + n^{7/6}t^{7/9}, m + m^{3/4}t^{1/2},$ $n^{3/2}t^{1/2})\bigr)$, improving upon the best known classical bounds over a broad range of parameters. Our algorithm is based on a heavy-light vertex partition and an extension of triangle detection via quantum walks and Grover search.   Leveraging this result, we design a quantum algorithm for constructing $\varepsilon$-triangle cut sparsifiers of size $\widetilde{O}(n/\varepsilon^2)$ in time $\widetilde{O}(T_{\mathrm{q\text{-}list}} + \sqrt{mn}/\varepsilon)$.   Finally, we demonstrate applications to clustering algorithms based on triangle-related measures   and prove a lower bound of $Ω(n/\varepsilon^2)$ on the size of any $\varepsilon$-triangle cut sparsifiers.
summary: This paper develops quantum algorithms for triangle cut sparsification, a method to compress graph representations while maintaining structural information related to triangles. It achieves significant theoretical speedups by first presenting an efficient quantum algorithm for listing all triangles and then using this primitive to construct the required $\epsilon$-sparsifier with near-optimal size bounds.
main_problem: The core problem is developing quantum algorithms for triangle cut sparsification, which aims to reduce graph size while preserving approximate triangle counts across all cuts.
main_result: The authors present a quantum algorithm that constructs an $\epsilon$-triangle cut sparsifier of size $	ilde{O}(n/\epsilon^2)$ in time $eO(T_{q	ext{-list}} + \sqrt{mn}/\epsilon)$, establishing near-tight sparsity bounds.
method: The approach combines advanced techniques: quantum triangle listing (using heavy-light partitioning, quantum walks, or Grover search) with a strength-based sampling procedure to construct the sparsifier iteratively.
model_or_system: The system is an undirected weighted graph $G = (V, E, w)$. The analysis focuses on preserving the 'triangle cut' measure across cuts $(S, V \setminus S)$ by constructing a reweighted subgraph $G'$.
key_observables: Triangle counts crossing a cut, $\epsilon$-cut preservation factor, and the size of the resulting sparsifier $|E'|$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes access to Quantum RAM (QRAM) and relies on the efficiency of quantum primitives like Q-TRIANGLELISTING.
figures_summary: Not specified in the notes, but the structure implies comparisons between classical/quantum time complexities for listing triangles and constructing sparsifiers.
paper_structure: The paper builds sequentially: first establishing a quantum algorithm for triangle listing ($T_{q	ext{-list}}$) using multiple techniques (heavy-light, quantum walks); second, applying this to the strength-based motif cut sparsification framework; and finally proving lower bounds on the required size of such sparsifiers.
why_interesting: While focused on graph theory/algorithms, the development of novel quantum primitives like Q-TRIANGLELISTING demonstrates advanced applications of quantum search (Grover) and quantum walks to complex combinatorial structures, which has parallels in simulating quantum many-body systems or analyzing network properties.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
